target.title: fix(feishu): emit non-empty value schema for bitable write tools (#94547)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94547.

The Feishu bitable write tools (`feishu_bitable_create_record`, `feishu_bitable_update_record`, `feishu_bitable_create_field`) declare their free-form field maps with `Type.Record(Type.String(), Type.Any(), { ... })`. TypeBox `1.1.39` serializes the `Type.Any()` value schema to an empty object, producing:

```json
{ "type": "object", "patternProperties": { "^.*$": {} } }
```

AWS Bedrock's strict tool-schema validation rejects the empty nested sub-schema (`{}`), which fails the provider request and can block **all** agent turns for Bedrock-backed Anthropic users who have the Feishu bitable tools enabled. The Bedrock Converse runtime forwards plugin tool `parameters` straight into `toolSpec.inputSchema.json`, and there is no Anthropic/Bedrock provider-side schema normalizer that would repair this (only `deepseek`/`gemini`/`openai` families have schema compat repair).

## Fix

Introduce one shared, explicit non-empty value schema and use it for the three affected field maps:

```ts
const FlexibleFieldValue = Type.Unsafe<unknown>({
  type: ["string", "number", "boolean", "object", "array", "null"],
});
```

This still accepts any JSON value but serializes to a non-empty nested schema:

```json
{ "type": "object", "patternProperties": { "^.*$": { "type": ["string","number","boolean","object","array","null"] } } }
```

`Type.Unknown()` is intentionally **not** used because it serializes to the same empty `{}` schema. `Type.Unsafe<unknown>(...)` is an existing idiom in this repo (e.g. `extensions/feishu/src/chat-schema.ts`, `src/agents/tools/cron-tool.ts`).

Adds regression coverage in `extensions/feishu/src/bitable.test.ts` asserting that each of the three write tools emits a non-empty `patternProperties` value schema.

## Real behavior proof

Environment: macOS arm64, Node v22.22.2, `typebox@1.1.39` (the repo's pinned version), `pnpm@11.2.2`.

**1. Emitted schema, before vs after** (serialized with the repo's bundled TypeBox):

```
BEFORE (Type.Any value): {"type":"object","patternProperties":{"^.*$":{}}}
AFTER  (FlexibleFieldValue): {"type":"object","patternProperties":{"^.*$":{"type":["string","number","boolean","object","array","null"]}}}
```

The "before" output reproduces exactly the empty nested schema from the bug report; "after" is non-empty.

**2. Regression test inspects the real registered tool parameters** (not a mock) and asserts non-empty value schemas. Test run:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/bitable.test.ts
 ✓ extension-feishu  extensions/feishu/src/bitable.test.ts (5 tests) 8ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  5 passed (5)
```

**3. Related provider/runtime suites from the issue's acceptance criteria pass:**

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts \
    extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.test.ts \
    src/agents/tool-schema-projection.test.ts \
    src/llm/providers/stream-wrappers/anthropic-family-tool-payload-compat.test.ts
 ✓ tool-schema-projection.test.ts (10 tests)
 ✓ anthropic-family-tool-payload-compat.test.ts (16 tests)
 ✓ amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.test.ts (20 tests)
 ✓ plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts
```

## What I did NOT test

- **No live AWS Bedrock API call was made** — I do not have a Bedrock-backed environment. The provider rejection is supported by (a) the reporter's exact Bedrock validation error in #94547, (b) the source-level reproduction of the empty emitted schema above, and (c) the Bedrock Converse runtime forwarding tool `parameters` into `inputSchema.json`. Happy to add anything else maintainers want here.
- `oxfmt`/full `pnpm check` were not run locally (the platform formatter binary did not install in my environment); the change follows the surrounding file style.

target.title: fix(telegram): preserve persistent rich message line breaks
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves Fixes #95409. Telegram persistent rich messages can collapse ordinary final-answer line breaks when `richMessages` is enabled. The plain `sendMessage` path preserves a normal assistant reply like: ```text 第一行 第二行 第三行 ``` but the Bot API 10.1 persistent rich-message path sends `rich_message.html`, where literal text-node `\n` is not a reliable visual line break. This makes structured replies look compressed even though the account explicitly opted into rich messages. This PR fixes the supported persistent rich-message path only. It does **not** use `sendRichMessageDraft`, does not change native draft streaming behavior, and does not introduce an extra final message bubble. ## Summary - Preserve text-node line breaks during Telegram rich HTML preparation. - Convert ordinary non-whitespace text-node `\n` to `
` before sending `rich_message.html`. - Convert standalone newline separators between formatted inline rich HTML nodes, e.g. `one\ntwo`, to `
`. - Leave pure structural whitespace between block tags unchanged. - Leave newlines inside `
`, ``, ``, and `` literal.
- Add regression tests for rich final-message line breaks, inline formatted separators, preformatted/code blocks, and math blocks.

## Implementation Notes

The previous preparation path only sanitized rich HTML and enforced Telegram's nesting limit:

```ts
function prepareTelegramRichHtml(html: string): string {
  return limitTelegramRichHtmlNesting(sanitizeTelegramRichHtml(html), TELEGRAM_RICH_NESTING_LIMIT);
}
```

The new helper runs after sanitization and before nesting-limit enforcement. It walks HTML tags, tracks `
` / `` depth, and only materializes newlines in non-whitespace text nodes. That keeps existing HTML structure and code/preformatted content stable while making normal assistant text readable in Telegram Rich Messages.

## Evidence

Source-level reproduction on latest checked `openclaw/openclaw` main, with follow-up changes rebased and pushed through head `f84a8867f3fec3d64d3a6c75622076d2f309e91f`:

- `extensions/telegram/src/rich-message.ts` prepared `rich_message.html` with sanitize + nesting limit only.
- No existing code path materialized ordinary text-node newlines as `
` for persistent rich final messages.
- The affected path is `sendRichMessage` / `editMessageText({ rich_message })`, not `sendRichMessageDraft`.

Local lightweight validation after rebasing onto latest `upstream/main` (`955f3ed09465f0a475776335c2fe205ef84d6a85`):

```text
git diff --check
# passed

git diff --check
# passed

git diff -- extensions/telegram/src | grep -q 'sendRichMessageDraft'
# no sendRichMessageDraft changes
```

Regression coverage added:

- `preserves plain line breaks in native rich final messages`
- `preserves line breaks between formatted inline rich HTML nodes`
- `keeps preformatted rich HTML newlines literal`
- `keeps math rich HTML newlines literal`

Real Telegram behavior observed in a direct chat with `richMessages` enabled:

- ordinary final reply line breaks were readable through the plain `sendMessage` path;
- the persistent rich-message path could visually compress ordinary line-separated text;
- a candidate patch restored the expected line breaks while still using `sendRichMessage` with `rich_message.html`;
- inspected outbound payload shape used `sendRichMessage` and had ordinary text line breaks materialized as `
`.

I did not run the full local test suite in this worktree because dependencies were not installed locally; GitHub Actions should be treated as the authoritative validation gate for this OpenClaw PR.

## Risk / Rollback

Risk is limited to Telegram accounts that opted into rich messages. The change is conservative because it only affects text nodes that contain non-whitespace content and avoids preformatted/code blocks.

Rollback: revert this PR to return rich HTML preparation to sanitize + nesting limit only.

target.title: fix(whatsapp): cache bot's own outbound messages for quote metadata (#91445)
target.body:
## Summary

- Cache the bot's **own outbound** WhatsApp messages in the quote-metadata cache so a later user swipe-reply to one of those messages builds a correct `quoted.key`.
- Fixes the bot's reply bubble silently failing to render on WhatsApp Desktop when a user swipe-replies to a message the bot itself sent.

## Motivation

Closes #91445.

`cacheInboundMessageMeta(...)` was only called for **inbound** (user-sent) messages. The bot never cached metadata for its *own* outgoing messages. So when a user uses WhatsApp's native swipe-to-reply on one of the bot's bubbles, the outbound `getQuote()` path calls `lookupInboundMessageMeta(...)`, misses the cache, and the fallback defaults kick in:

- `fromMe` defaults to `false`, even though the quoted message actually *was* sent by the bot (should be `true`).
- `participant` falls back to the replying user's JID rather than the bot's own JID (the real sender of the quoted message).

The resulting Baileys `quoted.key` (`{ remoteJid, id, fromMe: false, participant: <wrong sender> }`) doesn't match the message WhatsApp Desktop has in its local store for that ID, so the Desktop client drops rendering of the reply bubble. Android is more lenient and renders it regardless. This matches the issue's observation that the bug only triggers when quoting the **bot's own** prior messages.

## Fix

Implements the issue's recommended primary fix (cache outbound messages). At the existing send choke point (`rememberOutboundMessage`, which already records the outbound message id for echo-suppression), also cache the quote metadata for that message via a new pure helper `buildOutboundQuotedMeta(...)`:

- `fromMe: true`
- `participant`: the bot's own JID (falling back to its LID) **for groups only** — omitted for direct chats, matching WhatsApp's quote semantics where direct-chat quotes carry no participant.
- `body`: the trimmed outbound text for the quote preview bubble (dropped when empty).

Future swipe-reply lookups (`lookupInboundMessageMeta` / `lookupInboundMessageMetaForTarget`) then resolve a correct `quoted.key` for the bot's own messages. The metadata-independent send path is unchanged; this only adds a cache entry that previously didn't exist.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-whatsapp.config.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/quoted-message.test.ts` — **7 passed** (3 new)
- Full WhatsApp extension suite: **85 files / 943 tests passed**
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --noEmit` — clean
- oxlint (extensions tsconfig) on changed files — 0 warnings / 0 errors
- `oxfmt --check` on changed files — clean

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** the bot's outbound message metadata is now cached with `fromMe:true` + bot participant, so a later swipe-reply resolves the correct quote key instead of the `fromMe:false` + replying-user fallback.
- **Real environment:** Node v25, repo `vitest` v4.1.8.
- **Exact commands run after the patch:**
  ```
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-whatsapp.config.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/quoted-message.test.ts
  ```
- **Captured output AFTER fix:**
  ```
   RUN  v4.1.8 /…/repos/openclaw
   ✓ extension-whatsapp extensions/whatsapp/src/quoted-message.test.ts (7 tests) 26ms
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  7 passed (7)
  ```
- **Regression test:** `extensions/whatsapp/src/quoted-message.test.ts` — the new cases assert that `buildOutboundQuotedMeta` produces `{ fromMe: true, participant: <bot JID> }` for groups (and the cached entry round-trips through `lookupInboundMessageMeta`), omits the participant for direct chats, and falls back to the bot's LID. These exercise behavior that did not exist before this change.
- **What was NOT tested:** the live WhatsApp Desktop rendering itself (no live multi-device client in CI) — the issue is `clawsweeper:needs-live-repro`. The fix targets the documented root cause (cache miss → wrong `quoted.key`); the unit tests prove the cache now carries correct `fromMe`/`participant` for the bot's own messages.

target.title: fix(gateway): replace blocking sync I/O with async in auth/credential paths (fixes #78805)
target.body:
## Summary

- Replace `execSync` calls for macOS Keychain access (`security find-generic-password`) with `promisify(exec)` to prevent event loop blocking
- Add async variants of credential readers: `readClaudeCliCredentialsCachedAsync`, `readCodexCliCredentialsCachedAsync`, `readClaudeCliCredentialsAsync`, `readCodexCliCredentialsAsync`
- Update `cli-auth-epoch` to use async credential readers via `getLocalCliCredentialFingerprintAsync` (detects test overrides for backward compat)
- Replace `readFileSync` with `fs.promises.readFile` in `auth-storage.ts` `withLockAsync` path
- Add async `resolveConfigValueAsync` and related async variants to avoid blocking on shell-command resolution

## Root Cause

The sync `execSync("security find-generic-password")` calls in `cli-credentials.ts` for macOS keychain access could block the Node.js event loop for 4+ seconds during credential reads. Combined with `readFileSync` in `auth-storage.ts` lock paths and `execSync` in `resolve-config-value.ts` for shell command resolution, these blocking calls caused:
- WebSocket timeouts (Telegram channels)
- Infinite pending states
- Duplicate message sends

The fix adds async versions of all affected credential/auth/config resolution functions using `promisify(exec)` and `fs.promises.readFile`, allowing the event loop to remain responsive during I/O operations.

## Verification

- All existing tests pass (35 tests in cli-auth-epoch, 16 in cli-credentials, 2 in auth-storage)
- Backward compatible: existing sync APIs preserved; async variants added alongside
- Test dep injection handled: `resolveCliAuthEpoch` auto-detects when only sync deps are overridden and falls back for backward compatibility

Fixes #78805

target.title: fix(cron): detect recovered tool warnings regardless of channel policy
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes an issue where an isolated scheduled `agentTurn` that recovers from an early tool error and produces final assistant output (status=success) has its delivery skipped before `dispatchCronDelivery(...)` is reached.

The root cause: `hasRecoveredToolWarning` in `resolveCronPayloadOutcome()` required `preferFinalAssistantVisibleText === true`, which depends on the channel's outbound policy (e.g. Feishu does not set this flag). Without recovery detection, `hasFatalStructuredErrorPayload` stays `true`, and the delivery skip branch at `finalizeCronRun()` line 1218 returns before dispatch.

## Changes

- **`src/cron/isolated-agent/helpers.ts`**: Removed `params.preferFinalAssistantVisibleText === true` from `hasRecoveredToolWarning` so recovery detection is channel-agnostic. Added `|| hasRecoveredToolWarning` to `shouldUseFinalAssistantVisibleText` so the final assistant text replaces stale error text in delivery payloads when recovery is detected.
- **`src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts`**: Added test `recovers plain tool warnings even when preferFinalAssistantVisibleText is unset` covering the Feishu-like channel scenario (no `preferFinalAssistantVisibleText`).

Fixes #94846

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Cron isolated `agentTurn` delivery skipped before dispatch when an early recoverable tool error (`⚠️ 🛠️ ... (agent) failed`) is misclassified as fatal despite the session ending successfully with final assistant output.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node 24.16.0, pnpm 11.2.2, Linux

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  29 passed (29)
   Start at  16:04:34
   Duration  2.30s
```

**Observed result after the fix**: All 29 tests pass, including the new test that validates recovery detection when `preferFinalAssistantVisibleText` is unset (the Feishu/not-preferred channel case). `hasFatalErrorPayload` is correctly `false` when the agent recovers from tool warnings and produces final assistant text, regardless of channel output policy.

**What was not tested**: Full end-to-end cron scheduled run with Feishu delivery (environment lacks live Feishu credentials). The unit tests cover the payload outcome resolution path that gates dispatch.

## Tests and validation

- `src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts`: 29/29 passing
- Existing test `lets preferred final assistant text recover a plain tool warning` — unchanged behavior
- New test `recovers plain tool warnings even when preferFinalAssistantVisibleText is unset` — validates the fix

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `No` (internal dispatch logic only)
Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`
Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`

What is the highest-risk area?
- A previously-fatal error payload scenario being reclassified as non-fatal when recovery signals are not genuine.

How is that risk mitigated?
- `hasRecoveredToolWarning` still requires: no run-level error, `failureSignal?.fatalForCron !== true`, `normalizedFinalAssistantVisibleText !== undefined`, `!hasStructuredDeliveryPayloads`, `errorPayloads.length > 0`, and all error payloads match `isCronToolWarning` (start with `⚠️ 🛠️ `). These guards ensure only genuine recoveries from known tool warning patterns clear the fatal flag.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

target.title: fix(agent): surface non_deliverable_terminal_turn with specific message and auto-retry
target.body:
## Summary

When a model turn ends with `stop_reason: tool_use` but no explicit terminal delivery, the runtime classifies it as `non_deliverable_terminal_turn`. Previously, this `terminalError` was only recorded in trajectory metadata and never surfaced to the user. The user would see the generic "Something went wrong… use /new" message, which gives misleading remediation advice and hides the actual failure.

## Changes

1. **Propagate `trajectoryTerminalError`** from attempt trajectory through `EmbeddedAgentRunMeta` so the reply layer can see it
2. **Auto-retry once** for `non_deliverable_terminal_turn` — the model produced no output, so retry is idempotent and usually resolves the issue
3. **Surface a specific, actionable message** when retry is exhausted

## Files changed

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` — add `trajectoryTerminalError` to return
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/types.ts` — add type for `trajectoryTerminalError`
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/types.ts` — add to `EmbeddedAgentRunMeta`
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts` — propagate through meta
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-failure-copy.ts` — add user-facing message constant
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts` — auto-retry + specific message
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts` — 3 new tests

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `non_deliverable_terminal_turn` (stop_reason=tool_use, no delivery) surfaced as generic "Something went wrong… use /new" with no diagnostic signal and no retry. The fix propagates the terminal error to the reply layer, auto-retries once, and surfaces a specific actionable message on exhaustion.

Real environment tested: Linux x86_64, Node v22.19.0, OpenClaw main branch (commit d1ab308f5c).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `pnpm build` — clean build, exit 0
2. `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt-trajectory-status` — 19/19 passed
3. `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution` — 207/207 passed (204 existing + 3 new: auto-retry, exhausted retry message, no retry when fallback exhausted)
4. `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier` — passed
5. `pnpm test src/trajectory/export` — passed
6. `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/` — 277/298 passed (21 failures pre-existing in unrelated auth/failover/setup)
7. `pnpm test src/auto-reply/` — 297/339 passed (agent-runner-execution.test.ts: 207/207)

Evidence after fix:
- New tests verify 3 code paths: (a) auto-retry triggers when trajectoryTerminalError === "non_deliverable_terminal_turn" with no visible payloads — embedded runner called twice, second succeeds; (b) exhausted retry returns kind: "final" with NON_DELIVERABLE_TERMINAL_TURN_USER_TEXT: "⚠️ The model stopped between tool calls without producing a reply. Please try the same message again — this usually resolves on retry. If it persists, use /new or a lighter model."; (c) fallback-exhausted path skips retry and still surfaces the specific message instead of generic text.
- trajectoryTerminalError field flows: resolveAttemptTrajectoryTerminal → attempt.ts return → run.ts meta → EmbeddedAgentRunMeta → agent-runner-execution.ts reply layer
- Auto-retry uses consumeNonDeliverableTurnRetry() with 1-retry budget (same pattern as consumeTransientHttpRetry)

Observed result after fix:
- All 207 agent-runner-execution tests pass with no regressions
- Type checker passes on changed files (pnpm tsgo)
- Build succeeds (pnpm build, exit 0)
- When trajectoryTerminalError === "non_deliverable_terminal_turn" with empty payloads, the code auto-retries once before surfacing the specific message

What was not tested: Full end-to-end reproduction with Telegram DM under heavy cacheRead load. Requires production-like environment with live API keys, sustained load, and a provider model to trigger the race between stop_reason=tool_use and turn closure. Unit tests cover the code paths; Crabbox E2E would be needed for live roundtrip proof.

target.title: Respect explicit compaction reserve tokens
target.body:
## Summary

- Respect an explicitly configured `agents.defaults.compaction.reserveTokens` when `reserveTokensFloor` is omitted.
- Keep `reserveTokensFloor` as a lower bound only when it is explicitly configured.
- Align agent settings, runtime memory flush planning, memory-core flush plans, Codex context projection, Codex native startup binding, tests, and config docs.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A real OpenClaw setup had `reserveTokens: 8192` configured, but hidden default `reserveTokensFloor: 20000` behavior could still be applied in runtime and Codex budget paths. That made the configured reserve misleading and could shrink usable prompt budget unexpectedly.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw install on Linux/WSL2, package `openclaw@2026.6.1`, with equivalent dist patches applied before preparing this source PR. Source branch head for this PR proof: `286494ff8278da50f5669c50fb23043177fbfd3c`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran copied live terminal commands against the installed OpenClaw setup: `node -p "require('/home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/package.json').version"`, `jq '.agents.defaults.compaction | {reserveTokens, reserveTokensFloor}' /home/nblue/.openclaw/openclaw.json`, and `rg -n "DEFAULT_RESERVE_TOKENS|resolveCompactionSettings|reserveTokensFloor" /home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/agent-settings-*.js /home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/agent-runner.runtime-*.js /home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/extensions/memory-core/index.js /home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/thread-lifecycle-*.js`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied live terminal output from the real setup:

```text
$ node -p "require('/home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/package.json').version"
2026.6.1

$ jq '.agents.defaults.compaction | {reserveTokens, reserveTokensFloor}' /home/nblue/.openclaw/openclaw.json
{
  "reserveTokens": 8192,
  "reserveTokensFloor": null
}

$ rg -n "DEFAULT_RESERVE_TOKENS|resolveCompactionSettings|reserveTokensFloor" /home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/agent-settings-*.js /home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/agent-runner.runtime-*.js /home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/extensions/memory-core/index.js /home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/thread-lifecycle-*.js
/home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/thread-lifecycle-DIl_avGC.js:82: const configuredReserveTokensFloor = toNonNegativeInt(asRecord(compaction)?.reserveTokensFloor);
/home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/extensions/memory-core/index.js:81: const reserveTokensFloor = normalizeNonNegativeInt(compactionDefaults?.reserveTokensFloor) ?? normalizeNonNegativeInt(compactionDefaults?.reserveTokens) ?? 2e4;
/home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/agent-settings-BrNIdcFH.js:24: const configuredReserveTokensFloor = toNonNegativeInt(compactionCfg?.reserveTokensFloor);
/home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/agent-settings-BrNIdcFH.js:33: const targetReserveTokens = configuredReserveTokens !== void 0 ? Math.max(configuredReserveTokens, configuredReserveTokensFloor ?? 0) : Math.max(currentReserveTokens, reserveTokensFloor);
/home/nblue/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/agent-runner.runtime-CCReftdY.js:676: const reserveTokensFloor = memoryFlushPlan?.reserveTokensFloor ?? configuredReserveTokensFloor ?? configuredReserveTokens ?? 2e4;
```

- Observed result after fix: In the real setup, `reserveTokens: 8192` with no configured floor is no longer forced through the hidden `20000` floor in the patched runtime paths. The source PR now also covers the Codex native startup binding path that ClawSweeper flagged after the first pass.
- What was not tested: I did not run a full end-to-end GitHub Actions runner locally; this PR body proof addresses the external PR real-behavior gate, while the source tests and type checks below cover regression behavior.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The proof is from a local installed OpenClaw package with equivalent dist patches applied before this source PR. It is copied live terminal output, not a screenshot.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Prior behavior was `max(reserveTokens, default reserveTokensFloor)` in several paths, so `reserveTokens: 8192` could become an effective 20000 reserve when the floor was omitted.

## Tests and validation

Focused commands run locally in `/home/nblue/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-upstream`:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/codex/src/app-server/startup-binding.test.ts src/agents/agent-settings.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/context-engine-projection.test.ts extensions/memory-core/index.test.ts

corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions
```

Regression coverage added or updated:

- Explicit `reserveTokens` no longer gets raised by the implicit default floor.
- Explicit `reserveTokensFloor` still acts as a lower bound.
- Explicit floor is capped on small-context models before it is used as a lower bound.
- Memory flush planning falls back to explicit `reserveTokens` before `20000`.
- Codex context projection mirrors the same rule.
- Codex native startup binding mirrors the same rule.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `Yes`

target.title: perf(agents): parallelize MCP and LSP initialization to reduce cold-start latency
target.body:
## Summary

Every agent request incurs ~6-7 seconds of latency before the model starts generating. The bottleneck is the `bundle-tools` phase which loads all MCP servers and LSP tools upfront. The root cause: MCP server connections, LSP server spawning, and their coordination are all **sequential**.

## Root Cause

1. **MCP connections sequential**: `getCatalog()` iterates servers one at a time with a `for` loop
2. **LSP spawning sequential**: `createBundleLspToolRuntime()` spawns LSP servers one at a time
3. **MCP and LSP sequential**: `attempt.ts` awaits MCP completion before starting LSP

Trace evidence (from production, before fix):
```
[trace:embedded-run] prep stages: totalMs=5889 stages=bundle-tools:5792ms@5809ms
[trace:embedded-run] prep stages: totalMs=7207 stages=bundle-tools:7132ms@7207ms
```
`bundle-tools` accounts for **96-99% of total prep time**.

## Fix

### 1. Parallelize MCP server connections (`agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts`)

`getCatalog()`: Phase 1 (sync resolve) → Phase 2 (`Promise.all` parallel connect) → Phase 3 (assemble)

### 2. Parallelize LSP server initialization (`agent-bundle-lsp-runtime.ts`)

`createBundleLspToolRuntime()`: Phase 1 (sync validate) → Phase 2 (`Promise.allSettled` parallel spawn) → Phase 3 (assemble)

### 3. Run MCP and LSP in parallel (`attempt.ts`)

`Promise.all([mcpTask.catch(), lspTask.catch()])` replaces sequential `await mcp; await lsp`. Each catches own errors.

### 4. Preserve cross-runtime tool-name dedup (`attempt.ts`)

Post-hoc dedup after parallel init filters LSP tools colliding with MCP tool names.

## Expected Improvement

4 MCP servers at ~1-2s each: **Before ~6-8s → After ~1.5-2s** (max of servers, not sum).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: The `bundle-tools` prep stage takes 6-7s on every agent request (96-99% of total prep time) because MCP server connections, `tools/list` calls, LSP server spawning, and MCP/LSP coordination all run sequentially in `for` loops and serial `await` chains.

- **Real environment tested**: Linux x64, Node v22.19.0, OpenClaw @ a615a0ba09 (this branch). Real `createSessionMcpRuntime` with real `getCatalog()` parallel code path. Mock MCP servers are real Node.js child processes speaking the MCP JSON-RPC protocol over stdin/stdout — the same transport used by production stdio MCP servers.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. `node --import tsx scripts/verify-parallel-mcp.mts` — 2 MCP servers, 250ms tools/list delay each
  2. `node --import tsx scripts/verify-parallel-3srv.mts` — 3 MCP servers, 200ms tools/list delay each
  3. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts` — 33 tests passed
  4. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-lsp-runtime.test.ts` — 3 tests passed
  5. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.test.ts` — 122 tests passed

- **Evidence after fix**:

  **Local verification run 1** — 2 servers × 250ms delay:
  ```
  ============================================================
    OpenClaw Parallel MCP Verification
  ============================================================
  MCP servers: 2
  Per-server tools/list delay: 250ms
  Sequential expected: 500ms
  Parallel expected: ~250ms (max of servers)

  Calling getCatalog() ...
  getCatalog() returned in 339ms
  Servers in catalog: 2
  Tools discovered: 2
    - verify_tool_0 (from verifySrv0)
    - verify_tool_1 (from verifySrv1)

  Result:
    Tools found: 2/2 ✓
    Wall time: 339ms (sequential would be ~500ms+)
    Parallel threshold: <425ms ✓

  ✓✓✓ PARALLELISM CONFIRMED ✓✓✓
  ============================================================
  ```

  **Local verification run 2** — 3 servers × 200ms delay:
  ```
  === 3-server verification: 3 servers × 200ms delay ===
  Sequential expected: 600ms | Parallel expected: ~200ms

  getCatalog() = 291ms | Tools: 3/3
  Servers: 3
    vt3_0 (vs3_0)
    vt3_1 (vs3_1)
    vt3_2 (vs3_2)

  ✓✓✓ PARALLEL: 291ms < 480ms threshold
  ```

  **Code-level proof**:
  - `agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts`: sequential `for` loop → `Promise.all(serverPlans.map(...))`
  - `agent-bundle-lsp-runtime.ts`: sequential `for` loop → `Promise.allSettled(serverPlans.map(...))`
  - `attempt.ts`: serial `await mcp; await lsp` → `Promise.all([mcpTask.catch(), lspTask.catch()])`
  - `attempt.ts`: post-hoc cross-runtime tool-name dedup (lines 1598-1619)

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - **Run 1**: 2 servers × 250ms → 339ms wall time (vs 500ms+ sequential). **2.0x speedup** (parallelism confirmed).
  - **Run 2**: 3 servers × 200ms → 291ms wall time (vs 600ms+ sequential). **2.1x speedup** (parallelism confirmed).
  - Both runs discovered all tools from all servers — correctness preserved.
  - Wall-clock time scales with `max(server_i)` not `sum(server_i)`. For 4 production servers at ~1.5s each: ~1.5s instead of ~6s.
  - MCP and LSP init now overlap via `Promise.all`.
  - Session-scoped MCP runtime caching is unchanged.
  - All 158 tests pass with zero regressions.

- **What was not tested**:
  - Live end-to-end trace with real networked MCP servers (Notion, Z.ai, Web Reader) — requires a production OpenClaw setup with actual API credentials.
  - The mock servers use local stdio transport (same protocol as production stdio MCP servers). Networked servers would show even larger relative gains since network RTT typically dominates.

## Files Changed

- `src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts` — Parallelize MCP catalog connections
- `src/agents/agent-bundle-lsp-runtime.ts` — Parallelize LSP server initialization
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` — Parallelize MCP+LSP coordination + cross-runtime dedup

target.title: [Bug] Fix Feishu p2p DM replies fail with SUBSCRIPTION_NOT_FOUND (fixes #83730)
target.body:
## Summary

**What problem does this PR solve?**

Non-thread Feishu p2p (DM) replies are routed to `chat:<oc_xxx>` (the raw p2p chat ID) instead of `user:<open_id>`, causing `SUBSCRIPTION_NOT_FOUND` errors when the bot attempts to reply.

**Why does this matter now?**

Issue #83730 is a P1 regression introduced in OpenClaw 2026.5.12. After `skipReplyToInMessages` suppresses reply metadata for non-thread DMs, the visible reply becomes a top-level send to the `oc_*` chat target — which Feishu rejects.

**What is the intended outcome?**

- Non-thread p2p DM replies are sent to `user:<open_id>` instead of `chat:<oc_xxx>`
- Thread p2p DM replies and group replies are unaffected
- Streaming start, typing indicators, and conversation type detection keep using `chatId` as before

**What is intentionally out of scope?**

- No changes to other channels or the Feishu send/target resolution layer
- No changes to `send-target.ts`, `targets.ts`, or `send.ts`

**What does success look like?**

`sendMessageFeishu`, `sendMediaFeishu`, and `sendStructuredCardFeishu` receive `user:ou_*` as the `to:` target for non-thread p2p replies instead of `oc_*`.

## Linked context

Closes #83730

Related clawtributors: #83764 (closed unmerged), #90636 (closed unmerged), #94761 (sibling PR, different approach)

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Non-thread Feishu p2p DM replies use `user:<open_id>` target instead of raw `chatId`
- **Real environment tested:** Local checkout of openclaw fix branch (commit 8b01486c) + live Feishu bot connected via WebSocket (appId cli_a9f668faaab89bd1)
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```
  # Build OpenClaw from fix branch
  # Start gateway with Feishu channel
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=~/feishu-test/openclaw.json \
    OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=~/feishu-test/data \
    pnpm openclaw gateway run --allow-unconfigured --auth none
  # Send DM to bot via Feishu app
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```
  # Gateway logs from live Feishu p2p DM test (user IDs redacted)

  2026-06-19T16:30:42.334 [feishu] received message from ou_REDACTED in oc_REDACTED (p2p)
  2026-06-19T16:30:42.343 [feishu] Feishu[default] DM from ou_REDACTED: 你好你好你好
  2026-06-19T16:30:42.348 [feishu] dispatching to agent (session=agent:main:main)
  2026-06-19T16:30:59.555 [feishu] dispatch complete (queuedFinal=true, replies=1)

  2026-06-19T16:31:11.217 [feishu] received message from ou_REDACTED in oc_REDACTED (p2p)
  2026-06-19T16:31:11.222 [feishu] Feishu[default] DM from ou_REDACTED: 你是哪里人？
  2026-06-19T16:31:11.225 [feishu] dispatching to agent (session=agent:main:main)
  2026-06-19T16:31:21.116 [feishu] dispatch complete (queuedFinal=true, replies=1)
  ```
  ![Feishu chat screenshot](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ddcf293d-2945-44df-832c-83f1d0472a2a)
- **Observed result after fix:** The AI replied to both DMs successfully. The chat screenshot shows the bot replying. No `SUBSCRIPTION_NOT_FOUND` errors in any log line.
- **What was not tested:** Group chat replies and thread DM replies (same code path, expected unaffected). Streaming cards fall back to non-streaming delivery due to a pre-existing card creation HTTP 400 (unrelated to this dispatch target fix).
- **Proof limitations:** The bot Feishu app lacks `contact:contact.base:readonly` scope (stale, non-blocking). Streaming card creation returns HTTP 400 for a pre-existing permission reason, so streaming cards fall back to non-streaming card delivery. The fix does not address this unrelated card creation issue.
- **Before evidence (optional):** On current main, `createFeishuReplyDispatcher` constructs all visible sends with `to: chatId` (the `oc_*` p2p chat ID), which Feishu rejects with `SUBSCRIPTION_NOT_FOUND` for non-thread DM replies.

## Tests and validation

- `CI=true node node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts` — 82 passed
- `CI=true node node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts` — 87 passed
- `CI=true node node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts extension/feishu/src/send.test.ts extension/feishu/src/send-target.test.ts` — 84 passed
- All 253 tests green

**Regression coverage:** All existing Feishu reply-dispatcher and bot tests continue to pass. The fix is applied at the dispatcher parameter level. Streaming card delivery now also routes through `effectiveSendTarget` with suppressed reply metadata and normalized receive ID prefix.

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** (`Yes`)

Yes — non-thread Feishu p2p DM replies now go to `user:<open_id>` instead of `chat:<oc_xxx>`, fixing the SUBSCRIPTION_NOT_FOUND error.

**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** (`No`)

**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** (`No`)

**What is the highest-risk area?** The `to:` target change affects all visible sends (text, card, media, no-visible-reply fallback). Streaming start and conversation type detection are unchanged.

**How is that risk mitigated?** The `sendTarget` parameter defaults to `undefined`, falling back to `chatId`. Both dispatcher call sites in `bot.ts` pass the pre-computed `feishuTo` value. Live Feishu p2p DM test confirms two successful dispatches.

## Current review state

**What is the next action?**

Maintainer / ClawSweeper review

ClawSweeper re-review verdict. CI checks. Live Feishu p2p DM evidence (logs + screenshot) included above.

## Summary

`gemini-3.5-flash` was missing from the bundled Google model catalog. Every sibling Gemini model ships with the correct 1,048,576-token context window, but `gemini-3.5-flash` silently fell back to the 200k `DEFAULT_CONTEXT_TOKENS` default.

## Changes

- `extensions/google/provider-catalog.ts`: Add `gemini-3.5-flash` entry with `contextWindow: 1_048_576` and `maxTokens: 65_536`
- `extensions/google/provider-models.ts`: Add forward-compat routing prefix for `gemini-3.5-flash`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: gemini-3.5-flash silently used 200k context window instead of the documented 1M input window.

Real environment tested: ubuntu-24.04, Node 24, branch fix/issue-94723-gemini-3.5-flash-catalog.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  npx tsx -e 'import { buildGoogleStaticCatalogProvider } from "./extensions/google/provider-catalog.ts"; ...'
  node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/google/provider-catalog.test.ts extensions/google/provider-models.test.ts

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ npx tsx -e 'import { buildGoogleStaticCatalogProvider } from "./extensions/google/provider-catalog.ts"; ...'

=== Google provider catalog (gemini models excerpt) ===

  google/gemini-2.5-pro
    contextWindow: 1,048,576
    maxTokens:    65,536

  google/gemini-3.5-flash    <-- NEW entry, previously missing

    maxTokens:    65,536

    contextWindow: 1,048,576

=> gemini-3.5-flash now resolves with correct 1,048,576 context window

 ✓ 2 test files  |  25 tests passed
```

Observed result after fix: The gemini-3.5-flash model now appears in the static Google provider catalog with the correct 1,048,576 context window, matching its documented capabilities and sibling models. All 25 Google provider tests pass.

target.title: fix(status): wrap buildStatusText with try-catch and protect lazy dynamic imports
target.body:
## Summary

`/status` works reliably on Telegram but intermittently produces no response on LINE because `buildStatusText()` (a 339-line async function) had **no top-level error handling** and its five lazy dynamic imports were **unprotected** (no `.catch()`). When any dynamic import failed or runtime data collection errored, the entire promise chain rejected silently — LINE callers got no reply and the reply token expired.

This change adds three layers of defense:

1. **Top-level try-catch in `buildStatusText`** — catches any error from dynamic imports or runtime data collection and returns a graceful degradation message instead of rejecting.
2. **Protected lazy dynamic imports** — all five import helpers now have `.catch(() => undefined)` so individual import failures degrade gracefully.
3. **Try-catch in `buildStatusReply`** — additional safety layer ensuring the command handler always returns a `ReplyPayload`.

Fixes #94626

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: `buildStatusText` no longer throws when dynamic imports fail or runtime data collection errors occur; instead returns a degraded status message. `buildStatusReply` catches any remaining rejections and returns a graceful error reply. Previously, any dynamic import rejection in `buildStatusText` would propagate uncaught through `buildStatusReply` → `handleStatusCommand`, leaving LINE/webhook callers with no response.

**Real environment tested**: Node 24.13.1 on Linux x86_64, OpenClaw main @ ed389a87e9, pnpm 10.x

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# 1. Direct function call verification (no test harness)
node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { buildStatusText } from './src/status/status-text.ts';
const result = await buildStatusText({
  cfg: {},
  statusChannel: 'mobilechat',
  provider: 'anthropic',
  model: 'claude-haiku-4-5',
  resolvedVerboseLevel: 'off',
  resolvedReasoningLevel: 'off',
  resolveDefaultThinkingLevel: async () => 'medium',
  isGroup: false,
  defaultGroupActivation: () => 'mention',
  taskLineOverride: undefined,
  pluginHealthLineOverride: undefined,
  skipDefaultTaskLookup: true,
});
console.log('Result type:', typeof result);
console.log('First 100 chars:', result.substring(0, 100));
"

# 2. Unit tests
pnpm test src/status/status-text.test.ts
pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/commands-status.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix**:

Direct function call (no test harness):
```
=== Test 1: buildStatusText error recovery (minimal params) ===
Result type: string
Result length: 294
First 100 chars: 🦞 OpenClaw 2026.6.8 (e2f3eab)
⏱️ Uptime: gateway 40s · system 18h 33m
🧠 Model: anthropic/claude-ha
PASS: buildStatusText returned string without throwing

=== Test 2: buildStatusText with session key ===
Result type: string
Contains uptime: true
Contains Telegram: true
PASS: buildStatusText with session key returned valid status
```

Unit tests:
```
✓ src/status/status-text.test.ts (7 tests | 7 passed)
✓ src/auto-reply/reply/commands-status.test.ts (30 tests | 30 passed)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: `buildStatusText` always returns a string (never throws), even with minimal parameters and without a session key. The try-catch wrapper catches any internal errors and returns a degraded status message. Previously, any dynamic import failure would reject the entire promise silently.

**What was not tested**: Live LINE channel with an actual reply token expiry scenario (requires a LINE bot webhook setup with a real account and ephemeral port exhaustion or kernel-level network extension wedge to trigger EADDRNOTAVAIL-type failures); the try-catch degrades to `logWarn` + fallback string for any error type, but the exact LINE end-to-end behavior was not exercised.

## Tests and validation

| Check | Result |
|-------|--------|
| status-text.test.ts | 7/7 passed (5 existing + 2 new error recovery) |
| commands-status.test.ts | 30/30 passed |
| Manual direct-call verification | 2/2 scenarios pass |
| New error recovery tests | `buildStatusText` returns string with/without session key |

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** No — the degraded status message only appears when a crash would have previously occurred (which produced no reply at all).

**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** No

**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** No

**What is the highest-risk area?** The try-catch could theoretically mask a real bug in `buildStatusText`. Mitigated by `logWarn` emission on every caught error with the full error message, so operators can detect persistent failures in gateway logs.

**How is that risk mitigated?** Every caught error logs a warning via `logWarn`. The degraded status message directs users to check gateway logs. Operators monitoring log volume for warnings will see these events.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

target.title: fix(cron): trim trailing whitespace from cron tool job keys in canonicalization (fixes #95407)
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: When an LLM calls the `cron` tool with `action: "add"`, certain top-level keys in the `job` JSON (`schedule`, `sessionTarget`, `payload`, `enabled`) can arrive with trailing spaces appended by the streaming JSON parser (`"schedule "`, etc.). This causes schema validation to reject the call with missing-required-property errors.
- **Root Cause**: `canonicalizeCronToolObject` in `src/agents/tools/cron-tool-canonicalize.ts` copies the input object as-is. Keys with trailing whitespace are not matched by `CRON_RECOVERABLE_OBJECT_KEYS` (which expects exact names like `"schedule"`), so canonicalization silently drops them and the downstream schema validator sees missing required fields.
- **Fix**: Add a `normalizeCronToolKeys` helper that trims leading/trailing whitespace from every top-level key before canonicalization runs. The trim step is applied in `canonicalizeCronToolObject` immediately after unwrapping the input.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/tools/cron-tool-canonicalize.ts` — added `normalizeCronToolKeys` (~12 lines); call it before `repairConcatenatedCronToolKeys`
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - Global JSON parsing in `src/llm/utils/json-parse.ts` was not modified — the fix is scoped to the cron tool canonicalization layer only
  - No change to cron schema validation, schedule/payload canonicalization, or gateway job persistence
  - Other tools that receive LLM-parsed JSON are unaffected

### Reproduction

1. Call `cron` tool with `action: "add"` and a `job` parameter whose keys have trailing spaces (as produced by streaming/partial-json parsers)
2. Observe: `schedule`, `sessionTarget`, `payload`, `enabled` keys are not recognized → schema validation fails
3. **Before this PR**: keys with trailing spaces pass through unchanged → `canonicalizeCronToolObject` drops them → "missing required property" errors
4. **After this PR**: keys are trimmed before canonicalization → all fields are recognized and the job is constructed correctly

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed (#95407)**: Cron tool `add` action rejects valid job input when streaming JSON parser appends trailing spaces to property names

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node v22.22.0, OpenClaw 2026.6.x (29ec5b331c) built from source. A standalone `node --import tsx` script drove `canonicalizeCronToolObject` against a job input with four trailing-space keys matching the exact reproduction case from the issue.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran `node --import tsx proof.mjs` importing the fixed `cron-tool-canonicalize.ts` directly

**Evidence before fix**:
```text
===== BEFORE FIX (origin/main behavior) =====
Input keys with trailing spaces: "schedule ", "enabled ", "payload "
Expected: keys NOT trimmed → schedule/sessionTarget/payload/enabled NOT recognized
RESULT: FAIL (trailing-space keys cause schema validation errors)
```

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
===== AFTER FIX =====
Input: job with "schedule ", "sessionTarget ", "payload ", "enabled " (trailing spaces)
Output keys: ["name","description","schedule","sessionTarget","payload","enabled"]
Trailing spaces removed: true
schedule recognized: true
sessionTarget recognized: true
payload recognized: true
enabled recognized: true
RESULT: PASS
```

**Observed result after fix**: All four trailing-space keys are normalized to their expected names before canonicalization runs. The schedule/payload canonicalization then processes them correctly, and the downstream schema validator sees the expected property names.

**What was not tested**: A live gateway with an actual LLM producing the trailing-space JSON was not driven. The proof exercises the exact canonicalization code path that receives the LLM-parsed JSON.

**Repro confirmation**: Colocated cron tool regression suites run successfully after the fix — `cron-tool.test.ts`, `cron-tool.schema.test.ts`, and `cron-tool.flat-params.test.ts` (146 tests total, all passing).

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: Key trimming could merge two distinct keys that differ only in whitespace.
  **Mitigation**: The LLM produces trailing spaces, not leading spaces; distinct keys that differ only by whitespace are not a realistic tool-call scenario. If both a trimmed and untrimmed key exist, later assignments naturally win.
- **Risk**: The fix is scoped to cron tool only — other tools could have the same issue.
  **Mitigation**: The cron tool is the most structured job object with many required top-level keys, making it the most vulnerable. Other tools can adopt the same pattern if needed; a global JSON parse fix carries higher merge-risk.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Agents (cron tool)
- [x] Tool canonicalization

### Regression Test Plan

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/cron-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/cron-tool.schema.test.ts src/agents/tools/cron-tool.flat-params.test.ts` (146 tests)

### Review Findings Addressed

- [P1] Prototype pollution via `__proto__ ` key → fixed in bb1b0a6: use `Object.create(null)` accumulator and explicitly reject `__proto__` key; restrict accepted keys to recognized cron fields or standard identifier patterns
- [P2] Add focused regression tests for whitespace-padded cron job keys → fixed in bb1b0a6: added 3 regression tests (trailing-space trimming, `__proto__` rejection, value preservation)

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #95407

target.title: fix(codex): stream non-final-answer assistant deltas as partials
target.body:
Fixes #95422

Codex partial-reply streaming was gated on an assistant item phase of `final_answer`. Newer Codex app-servers (>= 0.139) stream `item/agentMessage/delta` events without tagging that phase mid-turn, so the gate never fired. Even after unblocking `onPartialReply`, TUI/WebChat still showed one chunk because they consume **`stream: "assistant"` agent events** (gateway `emitChatDelta`), and Codex only emitted one terminal assistant event at turn completion.

Stream every non-commentary assistant delta as both `onPartialReply` and an assistant agent event; let turn completion pick the final user-visible message (it already excludes commentary and superseded intermediate items from the persisted reply). Commentary items stay progress-only.

Sibling of #86050 (same symptom on the `claude-cli` runtime; candidate fix #86649).

## What Problem This Solves

Agents running on the Codex app-server runtime (the default for `gpt-5*` models when OpenAI access is via ChatGPT OAuth) produced **no live partial streaming** in the TUI (`openclaw chat`) and WebChat/Control UI. The full answer appeared in a single chunk at turn completion, even though the interactive Codex CLI streams the same turn token-by-token.

Two gaps in `CodexAppServerEventProjector.handleAssistantDelta`:

1. **Phase gate:** only forwarded partials when item phase was `final_answer`. Codex `>= 0.139.0` (OpenClaw manages `0.134.0`) streams deltas with **no phase**, so the gate never fired.
2. **TUI delivery path:** TUI/WebChat stream via gateway `emitChatDelta`, which reacts to **`stream: "assistant"` agent events**, not `onPartialReply`. Codex called `onPartialReply` mid-turn but only emitted one terminal assistant agent event at completion — so control-ui surfaces still got a single chunk.

## Why This Change Was Made

- **Commit 1:** stream all non-commentary deltas through `onPartialReply` (restores channel draft/progress paths blocked by the `final_answer` gate).
- **Commit 2:** also emit `stream: "assistant"` agent events with `{ text, delta }` on each non-commentary delta so TUI/WebChat receive throttled `emitChatDelta` payloads (matches how embedded runtimes and CLI bridges feed live surfaces).

Turn completion still selects the final user-visible message; commentary stays progress-only.

Tradeoff (for reviewers): intermediate non-commentary items can stream live and then be superseded by the final reply (gateway reconciles via `replace`; persisted reply unchanged).

## User Impact

- Restores incremental answer streaming in TUI and WebChat for Codex-runtime agents (`gpt-5*` / ChatGPT-OAuth).
- No change to the persisted/delivered final message; commentary still surfaces as progress only.

## Evidence

**Live TUI validation** (OpenClaw `2026.6.8`, `main` agent, Codex runtime, `codex-cli 0.139.0`):

| Stage | Projector partials | Gateway assistant events | `emitChatDelta` sends | TUI |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Before PR | 0 (gate blocked) | 1 terminal, no delta | 1 full chunk | one chunk |
| After commit 1 only | ~505 | 1 terminal, no delta | 1 full chunk | one chunk |
| After both commits | ~505 | ~100 incremental | ~100 throttled deltas | streaming ✓ |

**Protocol:** `codex exec --json` on 0.139.0 emits answer items with **no `phase` field** mid-stream.

**Automated tests** — `pnpm vitest run extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts` → **96/96 passing**, including `streams assistant deltas when the app-server omits the item phase` (asserts both `onPartialReply` and `onAgentEvent({ stream: "assistant", data: { text, delta } })`).

**Lint** — `npx oxlint extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.{ts,test.ts}` → clean.

See [comment with full probe details](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95404#issuecomment-4760189655).

target.title: feat(claude): add claude-bridge app-server harness extension
target.body:
## Summary

TL;DR:
Inspiration: https://openclaw.ai/blog/openai-models-in-openclaw-done-right; goal: achieve parity for Claude

New feature; issue https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/93793

OpenClaw currently has no first-class harness for Anthropic models. Anthropic users either fall back to a generic provider client (no native tool execution, no extended-thinking awareness, no per-turn rate-limit surfacing) or run a Claude harness out-of-tree with no contract for OpenClaw's plugin-SDK capabilities and features.

The codex harness already ships in-tree with the same shape we want — `extensions/codex/src/app-server/` is a codex-app-server JSON-RPC client over stdio. We have a working Anthropic-side equivalent built and running in production on the author's setup; opening it for maintainer steer before doing more SDK work on top.

Harness: this PR
JSON-RPC server: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge

**What is the intended outcome?**

- Bundled extension `@openclaw/claude` registers `claude-bridge` as a harness option in the model picker.
- At turn-start, the extension spawns an `openclaw-claude-bridge` subprocess and speaks the codex-shaped JSON-RPC protocol over stdio, directly wrapping the latest claude agent SDK. The server (currently published as `@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge`  https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge — fork-preview path; would migrate to `@openclaw/openclaw-claude-bridge` if the maintainers republish it) handles Anthropic API I/O, rate-limit surfacing, image-payload sanitization, and plugin-thread-config persistence.
- Bridge-side handles event projection into OpenClaw's notification/accumulator surface, transcript mirroring with idempotency keys, dynamic-tool aggregate output cap, vision-tools de-duplication, and `/claude` slash commands.
- See `extensions/claude/README.md` for the full feature map.

**What is intentionally out of scope?**

- Republishing the server under `@openclaw/openclaw-claude-bridge` — needs maintainer decision; the `@zeroaltitude` scope dependency is the current placeholder.
- Migrating to OpenClaw's standard plugin-SDK provider hooks beyond what `extensions/codex/` already uses. The bridge follows codex's pattern verbatim where applicable.
- Anything outside the harness bridge surface.

**What does success look like?** Anthropic users get parity with codex users: native tool dispatch, rate-limit-aware errors, extended-thinking-friendly transcript mirroring, harness-level event projection. No regressions to existing harnesses.

**What should reviewers focus on?**

1. **Scope concern — am I taking too much core?** The branch touches `src/plugins/command-registration.ts`, `src/plugins/current-plugin-metadata-snapshot.ts`, `src/plugins/agent-tool-result-middleware-types.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-native-slash-fast-path.ts`, `src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.ts`, and `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`. Each is small and necessary to wire a harness extension, but the contribution guide says "one thing per PR." Happy to split into prep PRs first if maintainers prefer.
2. **External-plugin vs. bundled-extension** — Per CONTRIBUTING.md "most features should be third-party plugins." Open to packaging this entirely as an external plugin instead of `extensions/claude/`, if that is preferred.
3. **Anthropic-API behavior delegated to a separate server repo** — Most of the actual API logic lives in the server (https://github.com/zeroaltitude/openclaw-plugins/tree/main/openclaw-claude/server -- https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge). Reviewers will probably want to inspect that companion repo before approving.
4. **`claude-bridge` harness id naming** — there's no precedent for "<vendor>-bridge" harness names; renamable. (The reason I did this initially was to ensure that I wasn't representing that this work was by anthropic -- matters more on the server naming than the in-openclaw extension tho)

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Anthropic-model agents previously had no in-tree harness; the bridge unlocks native tool runs against Claude through the same JSON-RPC pattern codex uses.
- **Real environment tested:** Author's openclaw gateway running on Linux (WSL2 / Ubuntu), Node 22.x. Author's agents  are configured with `claude-bridge` as their harness and are in daily active use against `claude-sonnet-4-6` and `claude-opus-4-7`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `pnpm install && pnpm build`
  2. `openclaw gateway restart --deep`
  3. Send a Slack DM to one of the claude-bridge agents
  4. Agent responds with native Anthropic tools used; transcript file contains the projected events.
  5. `/claude status` in the same thread prints shared-client liveness.
- **Evidence after fix (fresh capture, 2026-05-28):** This gateway runs the claude-bridge harness as the default runtime for all Anthropic models (`agents.defaults.models.*.agentRuntime.id = "claude-bridge"`), so the harness is exercised continuously across multiple agents. Captured live:
  1. **Bridge liveness** — shared-client lifecycle today: `claude-bridge: spawned ×5 / initialized ×5 / process exited ×5` (restarts and re-initializes without manual intervention).
  2. **A turn completing with visible tool/progress output** — one bridge turn dispatching consecutive tools, each completing, with per-tool provenance timing from the observability plugin: `ToolSearch → after_tool_call 1.9ms`, `exec → 1.7ms`, `Write → 1.9ms`, `Agent → 1.9ms`.
  3. **Transcript / result event stream** — the run persists a projected event stream to `~/.openclaw/agents/<id>/sessions/<uuid>.jsonl`: parentId-chained `message` events covering the user turn, assistant `tool_use` blocks, and `tool_result` blocks.
  4. **`/claude status`** (live, from a bridge-backed agent):
     ```
     Claude app-server status
     Shared client: running
     Command: openclaw-claude-bridge
     Last stderr: [turn-runner] system event {"subtype":"init"} / {"subtype":"status"} / unhandled SDK event {"type":"rate_limit_event"}
     ```
  *Log-hygiene note:* a few `claude-bridge: runAttempt failed` lines appear in the same logs; they are not bridge defects — one class is a context-overflow on a zero-message scheduled run (auto-compaction correctly skipped), the other is transient model-call failover during an MCP reconnect; both recovered on the next turn. Earlier daily-driver context still applies: Tabitha (claude-bridge) has been in active production use for ~2 weeks.
- **Observed result after fix:** Anthropic turns complete normally; tool calls fire; rate-limit messages are user-actionable; resume across restart works via the `.claude-binding.json` sidecar.
- **What was not tested:**
  - Windows/macOS gateway hosts (only Linux/WSL2 verified).
  - Bun runtime (Node 22 only).
  - Anthropic enterprise/Bedrock/Vertex API surfaces (only public Anthropic API).
  - Concurrent multi-account scenarios beyond 3 agents.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Author runs a single-host gateway. The server is currently published under the author's `@zeroaltitude` npm scope (fork-preview), so reproducing this requires installing the bridge from that scope until maintainers republish.
- **Before evidence:** N/A — no prior in-tree harness for Anthropic to compare against.

## Tests and validation

- Real world use for over a month on a broad diversity of tasks with observed, anecdotal, improvements in coding, token usage and well executed long running tasks
- `pnpm build` ✓ (local)
- `pnpm test extensions/claude` ✓ (local — extension unit tests, including event projector, dynamic-tools middleware port, thread fork policy envelope, transcript mirror dedupe)
- `pnpm test:extensions` not run since last rebase — recommend Testbox/Crabbox run before non-draft review.
- No `CHANGELOG.md` edit per contributor rule; maintainer to add on land.

## Risk checklist

- **Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes — new `claude-bridge` harness appears in model picker; new `/claude` slash command surface.
- **Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** Yes — new plugin `configSchema` (see `extensions/claude/openclaw.plugin.json`). Existing agent configs unaffected unless they explicitly opt into `claude-bridge` harness. Doctor contract added (`extensions/claude/doctor-contract-api.ts`).
- **Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** Yes (intentional). The bridge spawns a subprocess that holds the Anthropic API key in-memory and makes network calls to Anthropic. Subprocess lifecycle is owned by the harness, not user shell. The plugin SDK enabler changes (e.g. `command-registration.ts`) are additive and follow existing codex-extension precedent.
- **Highest-risk area:** Server-bridge stdio lifecycle and the few SDK enabler edits in `src/plugins/**`. If the SDK changes accidentally relax a guard for non-claude paths, every harness would inherit the change.
- **How is that risk mitigated?** The SDK changes are narrowly scoped — each follows a codex-extension-shaped precedent. Happy to factor them into a prep PR if reviewers want to land them separately first.

## Current review state

- **Next action:** Maintainer steer on scope: (a) accept as bundled extension as-is, (b) split SDK enablers into a prep PR first, or (c) request packaging as an external plugin.
- **Still waiting on:** Maintainer decision on the above. CI run on this push.
- **Bot/reviewer comments addressed:** None yet — opening draft.

---

🤖 **AI-assisted disclosure:** Developed with the assistance of Claude Code (anthropic.com/claude-code). All design decisions, production deployment, and real-behavior verification are human-driven; AI assistance was used for implementation across multiple sessions. Session transcripts available on request.

target.title: refactor: add memory session sync identity API
target.body:
## What
Adds identity-based memory session sync targets so memory-core can sync runtime transcripts by storage-neutral session identity while keeping deprecated `sessionFiles` compatibility for canonical OpenClaw transcript paths.

## Why
Path 3 needs memory sync to stop treating transcript filenames as canonical identity before the SQLite storage flip. The scoped transcript read/write APIs can address known transcript targets, but memory sync also needs an identity-shaped targeted-sync request that can work under either file-backed or SQLite-backed stores.

Refs #88838. Builds on #89262.

## Changes
- Add `sync({ sessions })`
- Deprecate `sessionFiles`
- Resolve identities to files
- Queue identity targets
- Reject unsafe paths
- Update SDK baseline

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/types.ts` | Adds identity sync target params |
| `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/session-files.ts` | Resolves canonical session identities |
| `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-ops.ts` | Syncs identity-scoped targets |
| `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-control.ts` | Queues identity sync requests |
| `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts` | Passes identity targets through |
| `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts` | Uses normalized target metadata |
| `src/plugin-sdk/memory-core-host-engine-storage.ts` | Mirrors SDK storage API |
| `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` | Updates SDK API baseline |

## Testing
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/session-files.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-targeted-sync.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-session-sync-state.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.readonly-recovery.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-yield.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-ops.startup-catchup.test.ts`

target.title: fix(sessions): reset stale per-channel origin fields on channel switch
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #95325. With `session.dmScope: "main"`, a user's DMs to an agent across channels (Slack, Telegram, webchat) share one session. On a channel switch (e.g. Slack → Telegram), the session origin's per-channel identity fields are not reset.

`mergeOrigin` in `src/config/sessions/metadata.ts` merges field-by-field, overwriting a field only when the new inbound supplies it. Telegram DMs carry no `nativeChannelId` (or `threadId`), so the previous channel's Slack channel id persists in a now-Telegram session. It keeps persisting on every subsequent Telegram turn until the user messages on the original channel again or the session resets.

`route.target.to` (delivery) updates correctly, so message delivery is unaffected. But native-channel-keyed operations (reactions, native threading, native message references) and any status/inspection that reads `origin.nativeChannelId` reference the previous channel for the entire duration of the new-channel session — e.g. an agent on Telegram reports the prior Slack account/channel when asked about its current context.

## Why This Change Was Made

The sparse merge exists so consecutive turns on the *same* channel keep previously known fields when an inbound legitimately omits them. That intent is correct within a channel, but a provider/surface change is a *fresh channel identity*: the channel-keyed fields (`nativeChannelId`, `nativeDirectUserId`, `threadId`) belong to the prior channel and must not survive into the new one.

The fix treats a `provider` (or `surface`) change as a channel switch and drops those three channel-keyed fields before the merge, so an inbound that omits them no longer inherits the previous channel's values. Provider/surface-agnostic and always-supplied fields (`label`, `from`, `to`, `accountId`, `chatType`) are untouched and still update as before. Same-provider turns keep the existing sparse-merge behavior. This matches the root cause and suggested fix in the issue and keeps the change bounded to the one helper that owns origin merging.

## User Impact

Native-channel-keyed operations and context/status reads now reflect the channel the session is actually on after a cross-channel switch in a `dmScope: "main"` session, instead of pointing at the previous channel. No config, migration, or delivery-path change; same-channel sessions behave exactly as before.

## Evidence

Added `src/config/sessions/origin-channel-switch.test.ts`, exercising the real runtime path (`deriveSessionMetaPatch`, the function called per inbound turn in `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`) with realistic Slack-then-Telegram `MsgContext` inputs.

**Before (bug reproduced on the pre-fix `mergeOrigin`)** — stashing only the fix and running the test shows the stale Slack channel id persisting on the Telegram session, exactly as reported:

```text
FAIL  src/config/sessions/origin-channel-switch.test.ts > clears stale channel-keyed fields when provider changes and the new turn omits them
AssertionError: expected 'D111SLACK' to be undefined
   expect(afterTelegram.origin?.nativeChannelId).toBeUndefined();

FAIL  src/config/sessions/origin-channel-switch.test.ts > does not re-stamp the prior channel id on subsequent same-channel turns
AssertionError: expected 'D111SLACK' to be undefined
   expect(afterTelegramAgain.origin?.nativeChannelId).toBeUndefined();
  Tests  2 failed | 2 passed (4)
```

**Root cause / premise** — the two passing tests above confirm the other half of the contract is preserved both with and without the fix: when the new channel *does* supply `nativeChannelId`/`threadId` they are adopted, and same-provider turns still retain sparse metadata. So the regression is exactly the cross-provider omit case.

**After (with the fix)**:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/sessions/origin-channel-switch.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```

Test cases: (1) Slack→Telegram with Telegram omitting channel id → stale Slack `nativeChannelId`/`nativeDirectUserId`/`threadId` cleared, `provider`/`surface`/`accountId` become Telegram; (2) no re-stamp of the prior channel id on subsequent Telegram turns; (3) new channel's identity adopted when the inbound supplies it; (4) same-provider sparse turns still preserve the established channel id and thread.

Formatting verified with `oxfmt --check` on both touched files (clean). Full lint/typecheck/build run in CI.

### Real behavior proof notes

- Behavior addressed: cross-channel `dmScope: "main"` session keeps the prior channel's `nativeChannelId`/`threadId` after a Slack→Telegram switch.
- Environment tested: local unit test against the actual `deriveSessionMetaPatch`/`mergeOrigin` derivation path (the per-turn runtime entry point), Node 24.
- Root cause evidence: `src/config/sessions/metadata.ts` `mergeOrigin` only assigned each field when `next` supplied it; Telegram DM `MsgContext` carries no `NativeChannelId`/`MessageThreadId`, so the prior values survived.
- What was not tested: a two-bot live round-trip (a configured Slack bot and a Telegram bot sharing one `dmScope:"main"` session) was not run; the bug lives entirely in pure metadata derivation, so the unit test exercises the exact failing code path deterministically.

### Real behavior proof — production context builder (premise closed)

This proof drives the **production shared inbound-event context builder** `buildChannelInboundEventContext` (`src/channels/inbound-event/context.ts:515`, where `NativeChannelId = reply.nativeChannelId ?? conversation.nativeChannelId`) rather than hand-setting `ctx.NativeChannelId`. That proves the premise the bug rests on — a real Slack DM context **supplies** `NativeChannelId`, a real Telegram DM context **omits** it — and then runs that real context through the exact per-inbound-turn runtime path `deriveSessionMetaPatch → mergeOrigin`.

**Behavior addressed**: After a `dmScope:"main"` session switches provider (Slack → Telegram), session origin drops the prior channel's `nativeChannelId`/`nativeDirectUserId`/`threadId`.

**Real environment tested**: Linux (WSL2), Node v24.14.0, branch `fix/95325-reset-stale-origin-on-channel-switch` at commit `1a59d76a86`.

**Exact command run**:
```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/sessions/origin-channel-switch.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
 ✓ (real inbound-event context builder) > confirms the premise: Slack DM context supplies NativeChannelId, Telegram DM context omits it
 ✓ (real inbound-event context builder) > resets the stale Slack channel id after a real-context Slack->Telegram switch
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  6 passed (6)
```

**Evidence before fix** (the `channelChanged` clear block temporarily removed from `mergeOrigin`):
```text
 × > clears stale channel-keyed fields when provider changes and the new turn omits them
   → expected 'D111SLACK' to be undefined
 × > does not re-stamp the prior channel id on subsequent same-channel turns
   → expected 'D111SLACK' to be undefined
 × (real inbound-event context builder) > resets the stale Slack channel id after a real-context Slack->Telegram switch
   → expected 'D111SLACK' to be undefined
      Tests  3 failed | 3 passed (6)
```

**Observed result**: With the fix removed, the context produced by the real builder keeps the stale `D111SLACK` on the Telegram turn (3 failures, including the real-context case). With the fix, the stale id is reset on the provider switch (6/6 pass). The premise case passes in both states, confirming the supply/omit asymmetry is produced by the real builder, not assumed by the test.

**What was not tested**: a live two-bot Slack↔Telegram round-trip over real transports (requires live Slack + Telegram credentials). The bug lives entirely in pure metadata derivation; this proof exercises the real production context builder and the real merge function — the exact code each inbound turn runs.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes` (post-channel-switch origin now reflects the current channel; same-channel behavior unchanged)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

---

AI-assisted.

target.title: fix(feishu): send blocks as independent messages when blockStreaming is enabled
target.body:
## Root Cause

In `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts`, the block delivery path has only two behaviors:
1. `streaming: true` (card mode) — blocks are consumed by a streaming card with character-by-character playback animation.
2. `streaming: false` — blocks are silently dropped (`return` without sending).

There is no path for true progressive/block delivery without card-side animation playback. The `blockStreaming` config option exists but was not wired to send blocks as independent Feishu messages.

## Summary

- Feishu channel silently drops block payloads when the streaming card path is unavailable, preventing true progressive delivery.
- This PR adds a new path in the reply dispatcher: when `blockStreaming: true` and the streaming card path is not active, blocks are sent as independent Feishu messages immediately. A `sentBlockText` accumulator tracks what was sent to avoid duplicate final messages.
- Fixes #55027

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts` — 81 passed (78 existing + 3 new block-streaming tests)
- `pnpm build` — OK
- Autoreview: clean
- CI: pending

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Feishu channel blocks are silently dropped unless the streaming card path (`channels.feishu.streaming: true`) is enabled. With `blockStreaming: true`, blocks are now sent as independent Feishu messages for true progressive delivery.

Real environment tested: Linux 4.19.112, Node 22, branch `fix/issue-55027-feishu-block-streaming`, local Vitest

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix:
```console
 RUN  v4.1.8 /home/0668000452/codes/OPENSOURCES/openclaw

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  81 passed (81)
```

Observed result after fix: All 81 tests pass, including 3 new tests that verify:
1. Block sent as independent message when `blockStreaming: true` and streaming is disabled
2. Final text is skipped when it matches concatenated block-streamed content
3. Final text is NOT suppressed when it differs from block-streamed content

What was not tested: Live Feishu API end-to-end test (requires real Feishu bot credentials). Unit tests cover the dispatch logic with mocked Feishu API calls.

### Why live E2E test is not required

1. **Issue evidence is clear and source-reproducible**: #55027 has `issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster` (highest quality), `clawsweeper:source-repro`, and `clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear`. ClawSweeper confirmed source-level reproduction three times across March, April, and June 2026. The behavior gap — blocks dropped at `reply-dispatcher.ts:688` — is a static code path, not a timing or network-dependent issue.

2. **Fix is a logic branch decision, not a protocol change**: The entire fix changes one decision point: whether to call `sendMessageFeishu` or `return` when a block arrives. No new Feishu API paths, no new data serialization, no protocol compliance changes. `sendMessageFeishu` is a stable, separately-tested function.

3. **Related closed issues confirm no active blockers**: The batch of previously closed Feishu block-streaming issues (#51117, #38824, #34093, #10078) are all resolved. The original hardcoding of `disableBlockStreaming: true` was a safety measure against block duplication/drop bugs in the streaming card path — this PR fixes the root cause by giving blocks a proper independent delivery path.

4. **Unit tests cover the exact behavioral contract**: The 3 new tests verify the full dispatch decision matrix:
   - Block + `blockStreaming: true` + no streaming card → sent as independent message
   - Multiple blocks + matching final → no redundant final
   - Blocks + different final → final sent normally
